one_line_takeaway: MAVM is a five-agent LLM pipeline plus a GPT-4o-curated vulnerability knowledge base that automates detect-confirm-repair-validate for recurring (cloned/forked) C vulnerabilities, beating patch-porting and LLM baselines by 31.9-45.2 points of end-to-end repair accuracy on a 78-CVE/114-function benchmark the authors built themselves.
key_finding: On the paper's own 78-CVE / 114-function-pair benchmark (13 C repositories), MAVM's detection-phase results (Table II) were TP=68, FP=21, FN=46, Precision=76.4%, Recall=59.6%, F1=67.0%, versus ReDeBug+Hash+FVF (TP=78, FP=219, FN=36, Precision=26.3%, Recall=68.4%, F1=38.0%) and ReDeBug+Hash+GPT-4o (TP=81, FP=112, FN=33, Precision=42.0%, Recall=71.1%, F1=52.8%). In repair (Table III), MAVM reached TPC/TP=51/68 (75.0%) and end-to-end Repair Accuracy=57.3%, versus six hybrid baselines combining ReDeBug+Hash+{FVF or GPT-4o} with {PPatHF, GPT-4o, or Mystique} for repair, whose accuracies ranged 12.1%-25.4% (TPC/TP 45.7%-60.5%) -- giving the paper's own headline claim of a 31.9-45.2 percentage-point accuracy improvement and 14.5-29.3 point TPC/TP improvement over baselines. Ablations (Table IV, RQ2) on GPT-4o-backed variants: removing the confirmation component (MAVM-c) raised FPs from 21 to 223 (precision 76.4% -> 26.6%) and cut Repair Accuracy from 57.3% to 17.8%, despite Recall rising to 71.1%; removing the VKB (MAVM-v) raised FPs from 21 to 32 (precision 76.4% -> 68.3%) and cut Repair Accuracy to 49.5%, with TPC/TP dropping 2.5 points. Both ablations are the paper's own controlled comparisons isolating MAVM's two novel components (VKB, confirmation agent) from the rest of the pipeline.
core_contribution: MAVM (Multi-Agent framework for end-to-end recurring Vulnerability Management), presented by the authors as the first system to automate the full recurring-vulnerability lifecycle -- detection, confirmation, repair, and validation -- in one pipeline. It integrates five agent roles (Porting Agent, Analyzing Agent, Consistency-Check Agent, Fixing Agent, Validation Agent) plus a Vulnerability Detector and a purpose-built Vulnerability Knowledge Base (VKB). Prior systems the paper cites (ReDeBug, the hash-based method of Tan et al./FVF, PPatHF, Mystique) are explicitly framed as fragmented point solutions covering only one phase each; MAVM's own contribution is the orchestration of these phases via LLM agents plus the VKB and a set of ripgrep/Tree-sitter-based context-retrieval tools that let agents pull function and struct definitions from both the source (original-CVE) and target repositories.
threat_model: The setting is recurring vulnerabilities that spread across code via reuse, forking, or shared logic (e.g., hard forks such as Vim/Neovim, or divergent branches of the same project such as FFmpeg master vs. release/0.5). Given a disclosed CVE (CVE ID + git commit URL) in a source repository, the system's job is to determine whether the same flaw recurs in a target repository/version, and if so, to locate it and generate a correct patch. This is a defensive/remediation workflow (detect-confirm-repair-validate for known, already-disclosed vulnerability classes), not an offensive/exploit-generation setting -- there is no adversary model, attacker capability enumeration, or exploitability analysis in the paper.
limitations: Self-reported by the authors in the Threat-to-Validity section: (1) Internal validity -- LLM output randomness was only partially controlled (temperature=0, fixed prompts), and due to API cost the number of experimental runs was limited, so no repeated-run variance analysis is reported. (2) External validity -- evaluation is restricted to C code only (the authors state the architecture is 'theoretically language-agnostic' but this is untested); the benchmark (78 CVEs / 114 function pairs across 13 repos) is described by the authors themselves as 'relatively small' due to the cost of constructing full end-to-end vulnerability-management data. Additional limitations evident from the method description itself (not flagged by the authors as limitations per se): the VKB's analysis reports and analysis points are both generated by the same LLM (GPT-4o) that is also used as MAVM's backbone and as a baseline, so detection/confirmation quality is coupled to that one model's zero-shot reasoning; the confirmation strategy explicitly trades recall for precision (MAVM's recall, 59.6%, is lower than the GPT-4o-confirmation baseline's 71.1%), meaning MAVM by design misses more true vulnerabilities than a laxer baseline even though it produces far fewer false positives; the validation-feedback loop is capped at a single re-patch iteration 'for efficiency,' which could leave some repairable vulnerabilities unfixed after one failed attempt; and the Wireshark repository was excluded from the dataset specifically because its template-driven code caused severe similarity-based detection skew, indicating the approach's detection stage may generalize poorly to highly templated codebases.

datasets_used: Authors' own constructed benchmark: 78 CVE patch-migration cases / 114 function-level vulnerable-function pairs across 13 open-source C repositories (apache/httpd, FFmpeg/FFmpeg, FreeRDP/FreeRDP, krb5/krb5, xen-project/xen, OISF/suricata, openssl/openssl, OpenVPN/openvpn, python/cpython, qemu/qemu, sqlite/sqlite, vim/vim, neovim/neovim), built by filtering/refining the Mystique dataset (694 CVEs -> 48 non-trivial CVEs / 103 function pairs after removing 603 identical-patch cases and inaccessible commits) and extending it with the PPatHF data-collection methodology applied to Vim->Neovim CVE transplants up to April 21, 2025 (59 CVEs / 74 function pairs), followed by further filtering (removal of orphaned commits, exclusion of Wireshark due to template-driven code causing detection skew)
National Vulnerability Database (NVD) -- source of CVE descriptions and CWE categories for VKB construction
100 manually reviewed CVEs with expert-authored analysis reports (used only to derive the VKB's report template, not as an evaluation set)
quantitative_results: Detection (Table II): MAVM TP=68, FP=21, FN=46, Precision=76.4%, Recall=59.6%, F1=67.0%
Detection baseline ReDeBug+Hash+FVF: TP=78, FP=219, FN=36, Precision=26.3%, Recall=68.4%, F1=38.0%
Detection baseline ReDeBug+Hash+GPT-4o: TP=81, FP=112, FN=33, Precision=42.0%, Recall=71.1%, F1=52.8%
Repair baselines' Accuracy range: 12.1%-25.4% (six hybrid combinations of ReDeBug+Hash+{FVF,GPT-4o confirmation} x {PPatHF, GPT-4o, Mystique repair}); TPC/TP range 45.7%-60.5%
Paper's headline claim: MAVM outperforms baselines by 31.9-45.2 percentage points in Repair Accuracy and 14.5-29.3 points in TPC/TP
Ablation (Table IV): MAVM-c (no confirmation component) -- TP=81, FP=223, FN=33, Precision=26.6%, Recall=71.1%, F1=38.8%, TPC/TP=54/81 (66.7%), Accuracy=17.8%
Ablation (Table IV): MAVM-v (no VKB) -- TP=69, FP=32, FN=45, Precision=68.3%, Recall=60.5%, F1=64.2%, TPC/TP=50/69 (72.5%), Accuracy=49.5%
MAVM exceeds MAVM-v and MAVM-c in Repair Accuracy by 7.8 and 39.5 percentage points respectively
baselines_compared: FVF (Tan et al., ICSE 2025) -- similar-but-patched-case filter, compared on confirmation/detection
PPatHF (Pan et al., ISSTA 2024) -- fine-tuned StarCoder-based automated patch porting for hard forks, compared on repair
Mystique (Wu et al., FSE 2025) -- semantic+syntactic LLM-based patch porting, reconfigured with GPT-4o as its base LLM for fair comparison, compared on repair
GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) -- used directly as a zero-shot confirmation/repair baseline, and also as MAVM's own underlying LLM

ReDeBug (Jang et al., IEEE S&P 2012) and the hash-based method of Tan et al. -- used as the shared, standardized detection front-end for MAVM and every baseline combination (not compared as competitors but as a fixed common component)
